sentences.base_language: I have a big presentation tomorrow.
sentences.grammarNote:
**있어요** (isseoyo) covers both 'there is' and 'I have' in Korean: 발표가 있어요 = 'there is a presentation / I have a presentation.'
**중요하다** (jungyohada) = 'to be important' — the modifier form 중요한 describes the presentation: 중요한 발표 (important presentation).
**발표** (balpyo) = 'presentation / announcement' — 발 (發, to announce) + 표 (表, to express). Common in school and workplace contexts.
**내일** (naeil) = 'tomorrow' — a time noun that stands alone without a particle.

긴장되다 (ginжangdoeda) = 'to become tense/nervous'. It is a passive-feeling compound: 긴장 (tension) + 되다 (to become). Compare 긴장하다 (ginжanghada) which is slightly more active: 'to tense up / to get nervous'. Both are common but 긴장돼요 is slightly more emotional.

**뛰다** (ttwida) = 'to beat / to jump / to run' — a versatile verb: 심장이 뛰다 (heart beats), 뛰어가다 (run over there).
**빠르게** (ppareuge) = 'quickly / fast' — adverb form of 빠르다 (to be fast), made with -게: 빠르게 뛰다 (beat fast).
To describe a racing heart, Koreans also say 두근두근해요 (dugeun-dugeunhaeyo) — an onomatopoeic word imitating a pounding heartbeat.
**심장** (simjang) = heart (the organ) — distinct from 마음 (maeum) = heart in an emotional sense.

sentences.base_language: I have been preparing for weeks.
sentences.grammarNote: 동안 (dongan) means 'for (a duration of time)': 몇 주 동안 (for several weeks), 두 시간 동안 (for two hours). It always follows a time expression and describes how long something lasted.

sentences.base_language: My friend Hyun says I'll do great.
sentences.target_language: 친구 현이는 잘 할 거라고 해요.
sentences.romanized: Chingu hyeoni-neun jal hal georago haeyo.
sentences.literal: Friend Hyun[topic] well do will-saying say.
sentences.grammarNote: -ㄹ/을 거라고 하다 (indirect speech for future/belief): 잘 할 거라고 해요 = 'says that I will do well'. In Korean, reported speech uses a special quote ending. Future or opinion: -(으)ㄹ 거라고 / Past: -았/었다고 / Present: -ㄴ/는다고.

sentences.base_language: She says I have prepared very well.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-다고 하다** (dago hada) = reported/indirect speech: **했다고 해요** = 'says that (someone) did well'. The **-다고** attaches to the plain form of the verb.
Compare direct speech: **"잘 했어요"** → indirect: **잘 했다고 해요** ('says that you did well').
**준비를** = preparation + **를** object particle. **준비하다** (junbihada) = to prepare; **준비** alone is the noun.
**정말 잘** = 'really well': **정말** (really) + **잘** (well) — both adverbs stacked for emphasis.

sentences.base_language: Hyun, what if I make a mistake?
sentences.grammarNote:
**아** (a) after a name is the informal address particle used when the name ends in a consonant: 현아 (Hyun!). If the name ends in a vowel, use **야**: 민호야 (Minho!).
**실수하다** (silsuhada) = 'to make a mistake' — 실수 (失手, slip of the hand/error) + 하다.
**-(으)면 어떡해요?** = 'what do I do if...? / what if...?' — a very common worried question pattern. 어떡하다 is a contracted form of 어떻게 하다 (what to do).

sentences.base_language: Everyone makes mistakes. It's not a big deal.
sentences.grammarNote: 별거 아니에요 (byeolgeo anieyo) is a very natural phrase meaning 'it's no big deal / it's nothing'. 별것 = 'a special/particular thing'; 아니에요 = 'it is not'. Literally: 'it is not a particular/special thing'.

sentences.base_language: Take a deep breath and believe in yourself.
sentences.grammarNote:
**심호흡하다** (simhoheupada) = 'to take a deep breath' — 심 (深, deep) + 호흡 (呼吸, breath) + 하다.
**-고** connects two imperative clauses in sequence: 심호흡하고 믿어요 = 'take a deep breath and believe.'
**자신** (jasin) = 'oneself / self-confidence' — 자신을 믿다 = 'believe in yourself.' 자신 can also mean 'confidence': 자신이 있어요 (I'm confident).
**믿다** (mitda) = 'to trust / believe in' — polite present: 믿어요.

sentences.base_language: Thank you, Hyun. I feel a little better.
sentences.target_language: 고마워요, 현아. 조금 나아진 것 같아요.
sentences.romanized: Gomawoyo, Hyeona. Jogeum naajin geot gatayo.
sentences.literal: Thank-you, Hyun. A-little better-became thing seems.
sentences.grammarNote: -(으)ㄴ/는 것 같아요 expresses a soft opinion or impression: 나아진 것 같아요 = 'it seems like I've gotten a bit better'. It is used to avoid sounding too direct or certain. 것 같아요 is one of the most common soft-assertion patterns in Korean.

sentences.base_language: I will do my best tomorrow.
sentences.grammarNote:
**최선을 다하다** (choeseoneul dahada) = 'to do one's best' — a fixed expression. 최선 (最善, the best/utmost) + 을 다하다 (to put all of into). A very common phrase of resolve and encouragement in Korean.
**다하다** (dahada) = 'to exhaust / to put all of something into' — 다 (all/completely) + 하다.
**-(으)ㄹ 거예요** is the future tense: 다할 거예요 = 'will do (my best).' It expresses both intention and prediction.
Alternative motivational phrases: 힘내요! (cheer up!/do your best!), 파이팅! (fighting! — encouragement).
